questions.text: I prefer to submit work quickly and iterate based on feedback rather than perfecting it first.
I hold the view that most social signals are overinterpreted by people who look for them.
I do not naturally sense when a friend group has an inner circle and an outer circle.
I notice that when I feel safe and loved, the relief is so intense it's almost like sinking into warm water.
I experience emotions at a moderate volume — present but never overwhelming.
I maintain a consistent work environment because familiarity supports focus.
I believe that patience and collective input lead to wiser choices.
I believe that following through on commitments, even small ones, is a measure of character.
I feel that the depth of my engagement with others' suffering reflects the depth of my moral commitments.
I tend to cry easily — at movies, during conversations, sometimes for no clear reason.
I tend to fill gaps in any group without being asked—whatever is missing, I provide.
I hold the view that taking the best opportunity for myself is simply good judgment.
I feel that refining my personal aesthetic—in everything from clothing to home design—is a worthy pursuit.
I often set the vision and expect others to execute it.
I pick up on tension between colleagues even when they are being perfectly professional.
I feel most at ease when expectations are set collaboratively.
I feel that good leadership means creating conditions for the group to make the call.
I hold the view that the moral concern I carry for those who suffer is the quality that connects my personal ethics to the universal demands of human solidarity.
I believe that making others' lives easier is more rewarding than personal achievement.
I pick up on when a person's energy dims slightly after receiving a response that was not what they hoped for.
I often rewrite others' contributions to match my vision.
I find that I do not track the unspoken turn-taking order in group conversations.
I often feel emotionally wrung out by the end of the day.
I redesign my approach each time I encounter a new problem rather than applying a formula.
I think that the discipline of maintaining emotional distance during difficult situations is something I have worked hard to develop.
I feel that my system has a natural resilience that brings me back to center quickly.
I am not afraid to overrule others when the stakes are high.
I prefer to engage with difficult human situations by first constructing a clear picture of the facts before considering the feelings involved.
I feel that attending to my own desires while others have unmet needs is hard to justify.
I find that approaching painful situations with analytical distance helps me see solutions that emotional engagement would obscure.
I notice that my mood doesn't fluctuate much throughout the day.
I believe that rapid experimentation teaches you more than patient, linear study.
I tend to use unexpected free time for personal projects rather than reaching out to see if someone needs help.
I feel that the degree to which suffering shapes my moral priorities is a feature of my character that I value and want to preserve.
I pick up on when a compliment about one person is actually a veiled criticism of someone else present.
I feel unsettled when I realize I've forgotten something I was supposed to do.
